The event is produced by Mission Neighborhood Centers Inc. (MNC) as a fundraiser for youth, children, family and senior programs. The Cultural Arts Committee (CAC) of MNC together with the Carnaval Advisory Committee oversee the administration of the parade and festival, which contract with long-time Mission District activist Roberto Hernandez to run the event. Special events VIP Party at the de Young Museum The de Young Museum hosts the official Carnaval Kick Off Party the week before the festival and parade. This free event features energetic dancers, fabulous costumes and a preview of what's to come at the Mission District extravaganza. King and Queen Competition In April, Carnaval San Francisco embarks on a journey to select the annual King and Queen. Contestants vie for the crowns with performances in Carnaval costumes, sometimes accompanied by other dancers and musicians. The winners of the competition will be crowned as King and Queen of the Annual Carnaval San Francisco Parade and Festival held at the end of May. Contest winners serve as the Official Ambassadors at the Carnaval San Francisco Grand Parade and win a $500 cash prize. Are there a way to add "another DataMemberAttribute" that can be used for my other serialization scenario? A: No, basically. If you want to use the existing DataContractSerializer, you'll have to maintain a second version of your class or utilize some form of conditional logic within your code. Here are two common approaches you might consider when needing different serializations for the same object using `DataContractSerializer`: 1. Versioning with Different Classes: - Create separate classes that represent each serialization scenario. - These derived or related classes can selectively include properties and fields relevant to their specific use case, marked appropriately with `[DataMember]`. 2. Conditional Serialization Logic: - Use a single class but implement logic within your application code to determine which members should be serialized at runtime based on some context (e.g., flags). - This might involve custom serialization methods or using `IDataContractSurrogate` for more advanced control over the process. 3. Custom Data Contract Surrogates: - Implement a surrogate that can modify how objects are serialized and deserialized. - The `[DataContract]`, `[DataMember]`, etc., attributes on your class remain unchanged, but you manage which members to serialize/deserialize through custom logic in `IDataContractSurrogate`. 4. Dynamic DataMember Attributes: - While not directly supported by adding multiple sets of `[DataMember]` for the same property/field within a single context, dynamic behavior can be achieved using reflection and possibly with surrogate methods. 5. Using Known Types or Extensions (Advanced): - If your class is part of an inheritance hierarchy where different derived classes are used to represent various serialization contexts, you might use `[KnownType]` attributes. 6. Alternative Serialization Libraries: - Consider using other libraries like `Json.NET`, which offer more flexible ways for conditional or custom property inclusion/exclusion during the serialization process. Biography Peissel was the son of a French diplomat, raised in England after his father was posted to London, and able to speak English from early childhood. He later became fluent in several languages, including Tibetan. He studied for a year at Oxford University where he earned degrees that would set him on course towards becoming an ethnologist and explorer. During the 1960s, Peissel began his expeditions to Asia with particular focus on Tibet and its surrounding regions in the Himalayas. His work often involved studying local cultures, languages, religions, and geography of these remote areas which were not well-known or accessible at that time due to political restrictions. One notable aspect of Michel's career was his role as a cultural diplomat for France during sensitive periods when direct diplomatic relations with China (and thus Tibet) were limited. He used this unique position to gather valuable information about the region and its people, often acting under pseudonyms while conducting fieldwork in areas that Westerners rarely visited. His books not only provided academic insights but also captured vivid accounts of his adventures which made them popular among general readers as well as scholars interested in Asian cultures and history. Some examples include "The Ants' Gold: The Discovery of the Greek El Dorado" (1983) where he explored ancient trade routes between Greece, India, Tibet, China; or more specifically about Tibetan studies like his book on hidden valleys ("Tibet's Hidden Lamas") published in 2004. Throughout his career Peissel received numerous awards and honors for his contributions to anthropology and exploration. His work significantly contributed towards our understanding of the Himalayan region’s cultural heritage, history, linguistics, as well as geography before it faced significant changes due to modernization efforts across Asia during late twentieth century onwards. Peissel's legacy continues through both his written works that remain influential in academic circles today, and also via organizations he was affiliated with such as serving on the board of directors for Save Tibet groups aiming at preserving Tibetan cult

This exploitation of shared resources by prt^−^ individuals without contributing to the protease pool allows them to invade and establish within a population of prt^+^ individuals, a phenomenon often observed in evolutionary dynamics ([@bib11]). When the protease is encoded by a plasmid, its loss in a prt^+^ population is a common event, leading to the rapid emergence of prt^−^ mutants ([@bib17]). This phenomenon is consistent with the evolutionary dynamics of a plasmid-borne trait, where the loss of a beneficial trait in a population can result in a transient increase in the population of individuals lacking the trait, thus fostering the spread of the trait in the population ([@bib1]; [@bib7]). To study the dynamics of mixed populations of prt^+^ and prt^−^ in serial propagation experiments, we employed the iterated prisoner's dilemma (IPD) model, a classic tool in evolutionary game theory, to model the strategic interactions between the prt^+^ and prt^−^ strains. The IPD model captures the essential strategic aspects of a situation in which cooperation (protease production) and defection (not producing the protease) are the primary actions. In this model, the payoff of prt^+^ and prt^−^ strains depends on the proportion of cooperators (prt^+^) and defectors (prt^−^) in the population, reflecting the competition for the limited resources provided by the protease. In our experiments, the population of prt^+^ and prt^−^ strains evolves over several rounds of growth and division, and the dynamics of the population is characterized by the fractions of prt^+^ and prt^−^ strains, denoted by \( f^+ \) and \( f^- \) respectively, at the end of each round of propagation. [Transmission electron microscopic study on eosinophil degranulation in nasal mucosa in allergic rhinitis]. To observe the ultrastructure of eosinophil (EOS) degranulation in nasal mucosa in allergic rhinitis, nasal mucosa biopsies from patients with perennial allergic rhinitis (PAR, n = 13) pollinosis (PS, n = 9) subjects and normal controls (n = 3) were processed and the ultrastructure changes of EOS degranulation were examined under transmission electron microscope and routine histopathological observation. The ultrastructure of EOS degranulation in nasal mucosa was characterized by the presence of piecemeal degranulation, which involved the release of eosinophil granules from the cell through a process of vesiculotubular transport. In patients with PAR and PS, extensiveEOS degranulation was observed, accompanied by disruption of the cellular membrane and loss of intact granules. Furthermore, in areas surrounding EOS degranulation, prominent ultrastructural changes were noted, including edema, collagen fiber disorganization, and increased vascular permeability. These findings suggested that eosinophil degranulation plays a pivotal role in the pathogenesis of allergic rhinitis by promoting tissue damage and contributing to chronic inflammation. The degree ofEOS degranulation was significantly higher in patients with PAR compared to those with PS (P < 0.05), indicating different underlying mechanisms or severity levels between these two conditions. No significant EOS degranulation was observed in normal controls, underscoring the association of this process specifically with allergic responses.

Badefols-sur-Dordogne Badefols-sur-Dordogne () is a commune in the Dordogne department in southwestern France. The commune is situated on the Dordogne River. Name As its name suggests, the town is set on the edge of the Dordogne, on the left bank, downstream from the dam Mauzac. The name of the town is of Occitan origin, but its explanation is uncertain. It could come from badar (gawk) and mad (crazy), or bada foolish (bystander), whose nicknames were dressed the villageois The second part of the name naturally refers to the Dordogne, on the left bank of which stood the Village. History In Gallo-Roman times, a port was established on the Dordogne. The construction of the Castle of Badefols is before century the 10th century. The first known written records of the town date back to the 13th century in the forms Badafol and Badefol1. The town was named in 1864 Badefols.
